title: Vehicle Accidents Involving Radioactive Sealed Sources
narrationScript: A loaded exposure device rides in a crew truck nearly every working day, so a vehicle accident is the radiological emergency you are most likely to face. The device is engineered to survive a severe crash — Type B accident testing sees to that — but its condition is verified, never assumed, and only a survey meter can confirm the source is still shielded. Your actions run in a fixed order. Protect life first: give first aid and call nine-one-one, telling dispatch a radioactive sealed source is aboard. Then survey: approach with the meter, locate the device, and read its condition. Set the public boundary where readings fall to about two milliroentgen per hour, keep everyone outside it, and maintain constant surveillance — nothing and no one crosses or leaves. Notify your RSO from the scene and follow direction. If the source is unshielded, damaged, or missing, expand the boundary, search at a distance, and never touch a bare source. You control the scene; recovery belongs to qualified, RSO-directed personnel.

sections.heading: An Emergency Begins the Moment Control Is in Doubt
sections.body:
In industrial radiography, an **emergency** is any event in which control of a sealed source is lost — or cannot be confirmed. A transport vehicle crash, a fire, a source that will not retract, a device that cannot be found: each becomes an emergency the moment doubt appears, not when harm is proven. This module covers all of them; this lesson takes the one you are statistically most likely to face. A loaded exposure device rides in a crew truck to job sites nearly every working day, sharing the road with everyone else, so **vehicle accidents are among the most probable radiological emergencies in this trade**.

The response is never improvised. **10 CFR 34.45** requires every radiography licensee to maintain written **operating and emergency procedures** — including how to minimize exposure of people after an accident and whom to notify — and you are required to know them and carry them out. This lesson gives you the framework those procedures are built on, matched to your two jobs at any accident scene:

- **Act immediately** — protect life, find out where the source stands, and get help moving.
- **Control the scene** — establish a boundary, keep the public out, and hold it until qualified assistance takes over.

Your company's written procedures and your license conditions are the binding documents. Where they are more specific or more conservative than this lesson, they govern.

sections.heading: The Cargo Is Built for the Crash — Verify Anyway
sections.body:
Start with what is riding behind you. In legal transport configuration the source is **locked in its fully shielded position**, the device is **secured against movement**, the **shipping papers and written emergency-response information** required by the 49 CFR 172.600-series rules are within reach in the cab, and your **survey meter travels with the crew** — the one tool that can answer the only question that matters after a crash.

The equipment is engineered for exactly this event. Radiographic exposure devices must meet **ANSI N432** and travel in — or as — **Type B packages**, qualified against hypothetical accident conditions: a **30-foot drop onto an unyielding surface**, a **40-inch drop onto a steel puncture bar**, a **1,475 °F fully engulfing fire for 30 minutes**, and water immersion. Inside, the capsule itself is **special form** radioactive material, tested against impact, percussion, bending, and heat so it stays sealed even if it ends up outside its shield. The most likely survey result after a highway accident is therefore *normal — source shielded, device intact*.

But the stakes if it is not are absolute. A typical 100-curie iridium-192 source out of its shield produces **roughly 50 R/h at one meter** — the entire 5-rem annual occupational limit in about six minutes — and far higher dose rates close in. So nothing is assumed in either direction: **the meter, not the appearance of the wreckage, decides**.

sections.heading: First Minutes: People First, Then the Meter
sections.body:
The opening moves at an accident scene run in a fixed order:

1. **Protect life.** Give first aid and get emergency services rolling — and make sure the 911 dispatcher hears that a **radioactive sealed source is aboard**, so responders arrive informed. Lifesaving care is never delayed for radiological reasons: if a victim must be moved away from the cargo area and it is medically safe to do so, move them. Keep the gathering crowd away from the cargo area in the same breath.
2. **Get the survey meter.** It travels with you for this moment. Approach the vehicle slowly from a distance, meter on and in view, watching the reading rise as you close in.
3. **Locate the device and read its condition.** Is it in its tie-down? Is the lock intact? Are the readings around it consistent with a shielded source — the same order of magnitude you measured at the start of the trip? A normal survey means the source is contained; an abnormal one triggers the escalation covered later in this lesson.
4. **Arm the responders.** Hand the shipping papers and emergency-response information to the senior officer on scene — that paperwork exists for exactly this conversation.

Even with a clean survey, the scene stays controlled until your RSO directs otherwise.

sections.caption: Overhead view of a controlled accident scene: the boundary is set with the survey meter at about 2 mR/h, the radiographer holds the line under constant surveillance, and responders stage outside it.

sections.title: Never touch a bare source — not for one second
sections.body: If your search finds the source assembly outside the device — a capsule or pigtail lying on the pavement — **no one touches it with anything held in a hand**. Contact with an unshielded radiography source delivers tissue-destroying doses in **seconds**, and picking one up has cost workers fingers, hands, and lives in documented accidents. Fix its location **with the meter, from a distance**, expand the boundary around it, and wait. Recovery is a planned, RSO-directed operation with remote handling tools, shielding, and strict time limits — never a roadside improvisation.

sections.heading: Set the Boundary with the Meter — Then Hold It
sections.body:
Your second duty is the perimeter. Walk outward from the vehicle with the meter and find where the dose rate falls to about **2 mR/h** — at that line a member of the public cannot exceed the **2-millirem-in-any-one-hour** public dose limit of 10 CFR 20.1301 even standing against it, and many procedures draw the line lower still. **Start wide and shrink the line only as the meter allows — never the reverse.** If the meter was destroyed in the crash, hold a generous default — the DOT Emergency Response Guidebook directs responders to isolate **at least 25 meters (about 75 feet) in all directions**, and much farther when fire is involved — until a working instrument arrives.

Then hold what you set:

- **Constant surveillance.** Licensed material outside storage must be controlled and kept under constant surveillance (10 CFR 20.1802). You stay — at the line, where dose rates are low — until properly relieved by another authorized person.
- **No one crosses** — not the tow operator, not helpful bystanders, not souvenir hunters. Use rope, cones, flares, vehicles, and the police.
- **Nothing leaves.** Debris can hide a source assembly, so the wreck is not towed and the cargo is not picked through until radiation-safety personnel clear it.
- **Write names down.** Anyone who was close to the wreck before the line went up is identified for dose assessment.

sections.heading: The Three Bad Surveys: Damaged, Unshielded, or Missing
sections.body:
Escalation depends on what the meter tells you:

- **Device intact, readings high.** The crash may have displaced the source or its shielding inside the housing. Do not unlock, crank, or "fix" anything. Expand the boundary until readings at the line are acceptable, and report exactly what you measure to the RSO.
- **Device thrown from the vehicle or missing.** Search systematically with the meter — roadway, ditches, the debris field — letting the rising count rate lead you. A found device is controlled where it lies. A source that stays missing is a **lost-source emergency**: the licensee must make an **immediate telephone report** to the NRC or the Agreement State (10 CFR 20.2201), and police help hold the scene while the search widens.
- **Device crushed or burned.** Special-form capsules rarely breach, but a crushed or fire-exposed source is treated as **potentially leaking**: keep everyone from tracking through the debris, allow no eating, drinking, or smoking inside the line, and hold anyone — and anything — that contacted the wreckage for contamination monitoring.

Fire response has its own lesson in this module, as do the source that fails to retract during a shot and the full emergency call list. The constant across all of them: **distance, control, and qualified help — never improvisation**.

sections.caption: The six-step response card for a vehicle accident with a sealed source aboard — and the escalation path when the survey is not normal.
Who is notified from a vehicle-accident scene. The radiographer's calls are 911 and the RSO; regulatory reports flow through the licensee — and your written procedures govern.
rows: Immediately — the first call from the scene
Injuries and location, plus the fact that a radioactive sealed source is aboard; shipping papers and emergency-response information go to the senior responder
As soon as life-safety calls are made
Device condition, meter readings, boundary location, and names of anyone who was near the wreck — then follow the RSO's direction
Made by the licensee/RSO — not a call you make from the roadside
Immediate telephone report for a lost source or an exposure that may exceed reporting thresholds (10 CFR 20.2201–20.2202)
Made by the company at the earliest practical moment, with written follow-up
Required when fire, breakage, spillage, or suspected radioactive contamination involves the shipment (49 CFR 171.15–171.16)

figures.title: Vehicle accident scene control — overhead view

interactive.title: Accident Scene Decision Drill
interactive.intro: You are driving the crew truck back from a pipeline job with a loaded Ir-192 exposure device locked and secured in the cargo area. At a rural intersection, a pickup runs the stop sign and slams into your truck. Make the calls a trained radiographer makes.

steps.prompt: The other driver is bleeding and dazed, steam is venting from both hoods, and a small crowd is gathering. Your first move?
options: Climb into the cargo area immediately to check the projector
Give first aid and get 911 called — making sure dispatch hears that a radioactive sealed source is aboard — while keeping the crowd back from the cargo area
Call the RSO first; nothing happens until the RSO answers
Move your truck off the road so traffic can keep flowing
steps.feedback: Life safety leads, always. The device is engineered to survive a crash, so the injured driver — not the projector — is the first priority, and the dispatcher needs the radioactive-cargo warning so responders arrive informed. Crowd control starts in the same breath.

steps.prompt: EMS is rolling and the injured driver is being cared for. What comes next?
options: Retrieve your survey meter, approach the cargo area slowly from a distance watching the reading, locate the device, and verify the source is shielded
Assume the projector is fine — Type B packaging is built for worse than this
Unlock the device and cycle the source out and back to prove the mechanism still works
Start pulling debris out of the cargo area by hand to find the device faster
steps.feedback: Only the meter can confirm the source is still shielded — appearance proves nothing, and hands stay out of the debris until the radiological status is known. Approach from a distance and let the reading tell you whether it is safe to close in. Cycling the source is never an accident-scene diagnostic.

steps.prompt: Your meter climbs fast on the cargo side — far above any normal transport reading — and the device's lockbox is torn open. You cannot confirm the source is shielded. Now what?
options: Reach in and re-seat the source assembly quickly — under a minute of work
Keep moving closer until you can visually confirm where the source is
Back out, establish a boundary where readings fall to about 2 mR/h, keep everyone outside it, and call the RSO from outside the line
Drive the truck to the office storage vault before the situation gets worse
steps.feedback: Distance is your protection and the meter sets the line — about 2 mR/h at the boundary keeps the public within the hourly limit. Hands near an unshielded source cause the worst injuries in this trade, your eyes cannot see radiation at any range, and the vehicle does not move until radiation-safety personnel direct it.

steps.prompt: Police have traffic stopped, and a tow operator wants to clear the wreck. The officer asks how long this will take. Your answer?
options: Ten minutes — the tow can hook up now if the operator stays on the cab side
The wreck and every piece of debris stay inside the line until radiation-safety personnel confirm the source is controlled — and I stay here maintaining surveillance until I am relieved
The tow can take the truck if I ride along with my meter
As soon as my RSO answers the phone, the tow can proceed
steps.feedback: Licensed material outside storage requires control and constant surveillance (10 CFR 20.1802), and debris can hide a source assembly. Nothing leaves the scene, no one crosses the line, and only RSO-directed recovery — not a ringing phone or a tow schedule — ends the hold.

checkQuestions.question: Your transport vehicle is struck at an intersection. The other driver is seriously hurt, and your exposure device is somewhere in the damaged cargo area. Your first priority is to:
options: Survey the cargo area so the radiological status is known before anyone is treated
Call your RSO for instructions before doing anything else
Care for the injured and summon emergency services, keeping bystanders away from the cargo area
Search the roadway in case the source was thrown clear
checkQuestions.explanation: Life safety always leads. The device is engineered to survive a severe crash, so radiological assessment follows within minutes — but never instead of first aid and the 911 call, which also carries the warning that a radioactive sealed source is aboard.

checkQuestions.question: The only acceptable way to confirm the source is still safely shielded after an accident is:
options: A survey of the device and surrounding area with a working survey meter
A visual inspection showing the device looks undamaged
Checking that the lock is still engaged
Confirming the package was properly labeled before the trip
checkQuestions.explanation: A housing and lock can look intact while the source or its shielding is displaced inside, and radiation gives your senses no warning at any intensity. Only meter readings around the device — compared against normal transport levels — verify the shielded condition.

checkQuestions.question: You are setting the public boundary around the wreck. The line belongs:
options: At a fixed 25 feet from the vehicle in every case
Where your meter shows the dose rate has fallen to about 2 mR/h — consistent with the public limit of 2 mrem in any one hour — or farther if your procedures require it
Wherever the police cars happen to be parked
At the edge of the pavement, since the road surface stops the radiation
checkQuestions.explanation: The boundary is set by measured dose rate, not by a memorized distance: at about 2 mR/h, a member of the public standing at the line stays within the 2-mrem-in-any-one-hour limit of 10 CFR 20.1301. You start wide and shrink the line only as the meter allows.

checkQuestions.question: EMS has left with the injured, and you are alone with the wrecked vehicle and its source. You may leave the scene:
options: Once the boundary tape is up, because tape legally restricts access
After one hour, if no one has approached
As soon as you have photographed the scene for the report
Only when properly relieved by another authorized person, because licensed material outside storage requires control and constant surveillance
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 20.1802 requires licensed material that is not in storage to be controlled and kept under constant surveillance. Tape and cones do not satisfy that by themselves — a knowledgeable person holds the scene until properly relieved.

checkQuestions.question: Your meter search finds the source assembly lying on the road shoulder 40 feet from the truck. You should:
options: Grip it with pliers and return it to the device — a few seconds of handling is acceptable
Stay back, control the area at a distance, fix its location with the meter, and wait for RSO-directed recovery with remote handling tools and shielding
Cover it with your jacket so no one nearby is exposed
Move it off the traveled way so the road can reopen sooner
checkQuestions.explanation: An unshielded source delivers tissue-destroying doses to the hands in seconds — pliers and jackets shield essentially nothing. The source is located and controlled from a distance, and recovery is a planned operation with remote tooling, shielding, and time limits under RSO direction.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Radiation Safety Course Outline (Appendix A) §10.0–10.1 — emergency procedures; vehicle accidents involving radioactive sealed sources
10 CFR Part 34 — §34.45 operating and emergency procedures; your license and written procedures govern accident response
10 CFR Part 20 — §20.1301 public dose limits, §20.1802 control and constant surveillance of licensed material, §§20.2201–20.2202 reports of lost material and incidents
U.S. DOT Emergency Response Guidebook (radioactive materials guides) and 49 CFR §§171.15–171.16, 172.600–172.602 — emergency response information and hazmat incident notification
